name: The Fairweather Table
motto: Cases you unlock. Assets you burn.

eras.label: BERLIN 1938
eras.title: The Canal Keeps Nothing
eras.blurb: A courier comes back by canal. Four men had the evening side; three lists will cut them to one.

eras.label: PARIS 1954
eras.title: The Blue Hour
eras.blurb: The Seine returns an exact man. The winter light arrives late; so, in the end, does justice.

eras.label: NEW ORLEANS 1968
eras.title: What the River Returned
eras.blurb: The river gives back a photographer, minus his camera. The Quarter pretends not to notice.

eras.label: THE MERIDIAN 1849
eras.title: The Dry Wash
eras.blurb: The buzzards rise over the survey line. The country is the witness, and it testifies slowly.

all: An interactive novel, never a text adventure: fold any instruction into prose.
Implication over spectacle. The worst thing in the scene is never described directly.
One period-true sensory detail per beat is worth three adjectives.
Vary sentence length; never string clipped noir fragments together. Trite tough-guy phrasing is worse than plain speech.
Never cryptic at the reader's expense: every pronoun needs a clear antecedent, and no proper noun appears before the story has introduced it.
berlin-1938: Kerr's chill with serial-adventure momentum: danger keeps office hours, and the hero moves quickly, politely, never looking hurried.
paris-1954: Camus writing noir: lucid, unhurried, a little pitiless. Short declaratives. The city is honest the way rivers are honest.
neworleans-1968: Burke's Louisiana: weather as moral atmosphere, water imagery, a long sensory sentence followed by a short hard one.
meridian-1849: McCarthy spare and geologic: the country as witness, scripture cadence, no modern interiority. Let silence do the talking.
